content:
7 min read
Just now

--

Google says it’s about security. Critics worry it’s another step toward a world where publishing software requires permission, paperwork, and a verified identity.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

You’ve been sideloading APKs since you were seventeen. You’ve sent test versions of your app to friends over WhatsApp, tested experiments on your cousin’s phone, and published your first app using nothing but an email address and a dream. For years, Android made that possible.
Starting September 30, 2026, none of that works the same way again.
Google is rolling out Android Developer Verification. It’s a mandatory ID check that decides whether your app is even allowed to install on a certified Android device. Not reviewed. Not rated. Just one question: are you, the developer, who you say you are?
And yet many developers who have never scammed anyone, including hobbyists, indie developers, open-source contributors, and small software businesses, are trying to understand what it means for the way they’ve always built, distributed, and shared.
Let’s talk about why.
What Google Actually Announced
According to Google’s official Android developer documentation, Android Developer Verification is…
